title: Global ocean protection is no simple equation
gist: The '30x30' target (protect 30% of land and ocean by 2030) galvanises action but a percentage cannot tell whether protection is effective, legitimate, equitable or enduring. An ocean ethicist argues conservation framed as separation/exclusion has historically harmed Indigenous and coastal communities, and proposes 'guardianship' — an embedded, responsibility-based relationship — as a better frame, citing French Polynesia's marine-protection model that embeds protection in existing guardianship rather than excluding people.

insights.claim: The 30x30 target creates focus, urgency and accountability, but a percentage can only measure how much ocean is protected — not whether protection is effective, legitimate, equitable or enduring, nor whether it strengthens human-ocean relationships or merely redraws lines on a map.
insights.speaker: Larelle Bossi
insights.so_what: Headline coverage percentages overstate conservation success; blue-finance and MPA claims should be assessed on governance quality, not area enclosed.
insights.quote: A percentage can tell us how much ocean is protected... It cannot tell us whether protection is effective, legitimate, equitable, or enduring.

insights.claim: Modern conservation has often been understood through separation — identifying ecologically significant areas and restricting or removing human activity — which has not always strengthened community-ecosystem relationships and has sometimes excluded Indigenous peoples from traditional practices via measures designed without their participation.
insights.speaker: Larelle Bossi
insights.so_what: Exclusionary MPA design is a legitimacy and durability risk; conservation outcomes depend on how protection is governed, not just what is protected.
insights.quote: protection has often been understood through separation
insights.locator: For much of modern conservation history

insights.claim: Debates over Indigenous whaling rights in the Arctic illustrate how biodiversity protection and cultural continuity come into tension when governance frameworks fail to recognise longstanding relationships of guardianship.
insights.speaker: Larelle Bossi
insights.so_what: Cultural-continuity tensions are a recurring failure mode in protection design; a flag for legitimacy risk in any rights-based conservation scheme.
insights.locator: Debates surrounding Indigenous whaling rights in the Arctic

insights.claim: French Polynesia, while establishing one of the world's largest marine protected areas, moves beyond a binary of extraction versus exclusion by recognising traditional artisanal fishing within designated zones, embedding protection within existing relationships of guardianship rather than treating all human activity as incompatible with conservation.
insights.speaker: Larelle Bossi
insights.so_what: A scalable template combining large MPA scale with embedded community use; a model worth watching for whether legitimacy-led design improves durability.
insights.quote: rather than treating all human activity as incompatible with conservation
insights.locator: French Polynesia's recent marine protection announcements

insights.claim: For many small island states and coastal communities the ocean is not a resource, protected area or economic asset but a source of identity, responsibility, livelihood and belonging, maintained through customary governance and intergenerational obligations that predate contemporary conservation frameworks.
insights.speaker: Larelle Bossi
insights.so_what: Terrestrial-governance assumptions (rights, ownership, management) misfit ocean realities; designs ignoring customary guardianship carry higher failure risk.
insights.quote: the ocean cannot be reduced to a resource, a protected area, or an economic asset
insights.locator: Yet many coastal and island societies

insights.claim: Guardianship — a deeper ethical relationship than stewardship, beginning with responsibility rather than ownership or control — may be a more useful frame for ocean governance because currents, migrating species and pollution cross jurisdictions, so the ocean is not easily divided into isolated management units.
insights.speaker: Larelle Bossi
insights.so_what: Success should ultimately be measured by enduring responsibility and legitimacy across generations, not percentage enclosed — a reframing for evaluating ocean-protection and blue-finance initiatives.
insights.quote: Guardianship begins not with ownership or control, but with responsibility.
insights.locator: In this context, guardianship may offer a useful frame
